title: Very Deep Convolutional Networks for Large-Scale Image Recognition
summary:
Problem Solved: Early networks (e.g. AlexNet) relied on large, heterogeneous kernel grids (11x11, 5x5) which increased parameters and constrained structural depth.
Significance: Replaced by residual networks for backbones, but widely used in perceptual loss functions, style transfer, and feature matching.
keyContributions: Demonstrated that stacking multiple small 3x3 convolutions standardizes visual design and enables much deeper networks.
strengths: Uniform, clean design utilizing simple 3x3 conv structures
Pretrained models serve as excellent generic visual extractors
weaknesses: Extremely heavy parameter count (138M+ for VGG16)
High computational cost and slow training rates
legacy: Established depth as a primary design dimension, driving research towards standardizing filter grids.

title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
summary:
Problem Solved: As feedforward networks exceeded 20 layers, training errors rose sharply (degradation problem) because backpropagated gradients saturated or vanished.
Significance: ResNet50 is still the default baseline backbone and industry benchmark for standard computer vision tasks.
keyContributions: Introduced identity shortcut skip connections to bypass layer parameters, enabling deep models to compile and train without optimization saturation.
strengths: Allows training networks with 1000+ layers with stable convergence
Dramatically reduced parameter count compared to massive sequential networks like VGG
weaknesses: Adding layers increases runtime, which can require bottleneck optimizations
Identity shortcuts do not increase representational capacity directly
legacy: Skip connections are now a standard architectural element in almost all modern vision models and Transformers.

title: Identity Mappings in Deep Residual Networks
summary:
Problem Solved: In the original ResNet, activations passed through additions and ReLUs, modifying clean identity shortcuts and slightly hindering gradient flow in extreme depths.
Significance: Commonly chosen over ResNet V1 when training extremely deep models or custom variations.
keyContributions: Redesigned the residual block to place Batch Normalization and ReLU activations *before* the convolution (Pre-activation).
strengths: Ensures an completely clean identity path for gradients to flow uninterrupted
Reduces overfitting and improves classification accuracy over original ResNets
weaknesses: Requires slightly different hyperparameter adjustments for training stabilization
Marginally changes the API and activation extraction points
legacy: Popularized the pre-activation design layout, which is standard in many modern transformer models.

title: Densely Connected Convolutional Networks
summary:
Problem Solved: Information and gradients can dilute or wash out when passed through deep layers, and ResNet's identity additions can blend features redundantly.
Significance: Used frequently in medical imaging and segmentations where high-resolution low-level features are critical.
keyContributions: Connected each layer to every other layer in a feedforward block, concatenating feature maps instead of adding them.
strengths: Exceptional parameter efficiency (matches ResNet accuracy with half the parameters)
Continuous feature reuse ensures all layers access low-level and high-level features
weaknesses: Extremely high memory footprint because concatenated tensors must be held in VRAM buffers
Slower inference speed on older GPU architectures due to memory layout overhead
legacy: Demonstrated that dense feature reuse and explicit channel concatenation are viable alternatives to residual sums.

title: MobileNets: Efficient Convolutional Neural Networks for Mobile Vision Applications
summary:
Problem Solved: Standard convolutional layers are too computationally expensive and parameter-heavy for real-time mobile app and edge deployment.
Significance: Highly popular for mobile applications, real-time object detection, and background blur filters.
keyContributions: Introduced depthwise separable convolutions to build lightweight, low-latency visual models.
strengths: Reduces computation (FLOPs) and parameters by up to 9x with minimal accuracy drops
Extremely small file size (e.g. ~16MB), ideal for mobile app bundles
weaknesses: Reduced capacity leads to lower classification accuracy on highly complex datasets
Depthwise layers can be memory bandwidth bound on server GPUs
legacy: Pioneered mobile-first computer vision and popularized hardware-conscious CNN design.

title: Searching for MobileNetV3
summary:
Problem Solved: Manually tuning lightweight convolutional networks is slow and misses optimal micro-architectural block selections.
Significance: Default choice for edge AI classifiers, on-device face detectors, and smart camera vision.
keyContributions: Combined hardware-aware Neural Architecture Search (NAS) and NetAdapt algorithms with the hard-swish activation function.
strengths: State-of-the-art speed-accuracy tradeoff on mobile CPUs
Introduces squeeze-and-excitation attention blocks to prioritize channels
weaknesses: Inference speed benefits are CPU-centric and may not show on high-end desktop GPUs
High design complexity makes custom modifications difficult
legacy: Demonstrated that automated reinforcement learning search yields better edge networks than human designers.

title: Rethinking the Inception Architecture for Computer Vision
summary:
Problem Solved: Earlier architectures forced a uniform choice of kernel size, missing multi-scale spatial features and incurring high compute costs.
Significance: Used frequently in image generation algorithms (e.g. calculating Frechet Inception Distance - FID) and classic vision systems.
keyContributions: Introduced multi-branch Inception blocks with factorized convolutions (e.g. splitting 7x7 convs into 1x7 and 7x1) to reduce parameters.
strengths: Extracts multi-scale features (1x1, 3x3, 5x5) simultaneously in parallel branches
Highly parameter-efficient for its accuracy class
weaknesses: Complex branch structure can cause bottlenecks in memory access and slow execution
Difficult to scale and adapt for custom segmentation/detection heads
legacy: Popularized parallel multi-branch architectures and auxiliary training loss heads.

title: Xception: Deep Learning with Depthwise Separable Convolutions
summary:
Problem Solved: The original Inception architecture assumed spatial and cross-channel correlations should be mapped jointly, wasting representation capacity.
Significance: Still widely used as a feature extractor backbone for semantic segmentation networks like DeepLabV3.
keyContributions: Replaced standard Inception modules with depthwise separable convolutions, adding residual skip connections.
strengths: Better parameter efficiency and accuracy than standard Inception V3
Extremely clean, uniform sequential layout compared to messy inception branches
weaknesses: Can be slower to train on systems without optimized depthwise convolution kernels
Relatively heavy parameter size compared to MobileNet
legacy: Bridged the gap between high-capacity inception designs and lightweight depthwise separable convolutions.

title: EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for Convolutional Neural Networks
summary:
Problem Solved: CNNs were scaled up arbitrarily (e.g. only adding depth, or only adding width), wasting compute and missing optimal representations.
Significance: One of the most popular and robust CNN backbones for image classification, transfer learning, and feature extraction.
keyContributions: Formulated a compound scaling method to scale network depth, width, and input resolution using a fixed mathematical coefficient.
strengths: Exceptional accuracy-to-parameter ratio (8x parameter reduction for similar accuracy)
Modular, mathematically coherent family scaling (from B0 up to B7)
weaknesses: Very deep variants (B6/B7) are slow to train due to deep sequential dependencies
Requires advanced training recipes (like AutoAugment) to achieve target benchmarks
legacy: Established scientific scaling frameworks for deep architectures, displacing ad-hoc scaling guesswork.

title: Learning Transferable Architectures for Scalable Image Recognition
summary:
Problem Solved: Manual architectural engineering is bounded by human intuition, which misses optimal, non-intuitive layer combinations.
Significance: No longer widely used due to cell irregularity, but historically critical as the father of modern search architectures.
keyContributions: Designed cell-based neural search spaces (Normal Cells and Reduction Cells) optimized by reinforcement learning.
strengths: Discovered highly optimized visual cells that easily generalize across different tasks
Achieved state-of-the-art ImageNet accuracy at the time of publication
weaknesses: Extremely complex, irregular node layouts make hardware caching inefficient
Massive reinforcement learning search required 500+ GPUs to run
legacy: Pioneered Neural Architecture Search (NAS), accelerating the automated design of AI models.

title: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale
summary:
Problem Solved: CNNs rely on local translation invariance and local receptive fields, which limits their global context awareness and scaling capacity on massive datasets.
Significance: Serves as the foundation for modern vision backbones, generative vision models, and multi-modal models like CLIP.
keyContributions: Applied standard transformer self-attention blocks directly to non-overlapping image patches, showing that CNN-style inductive bias is not necessary at scale.
strengths: Captures global long-range relationships in the very first layers
Scales exceptionally well with massive datasets and computational budgets
weaknesses: Lacks inductive bias, requiring massive dataset pretraining or heavy data augmentation to generalize
Quadratic complexity with token sequence length limits high-resolution deployment
legacy: Launched the Vision Transformer revolution, shifting the computer vision backbone paradigm away from pure CNNs.

title: Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows
summary:
Problem Solved: Standard ViT uses global self-attention, which scales quadratically with pixel resolution, making it impractical for high-resolution tasks like object detection or segmentation.
Significance: Widely used as the state-of-the-art backbone for dense prediction tasks, including object detection (COCO) and semantic segmentation.
keyContributions: Introduced shifted local window attention and a hierarchical patch merging structure to reduce attention complexity from quadratic to linear, enabling high-resolution image processing.
strengths: Linear complexity makes it fast and practical for high-resolution images
Hierarchical representations allow multi-scale feature extractions
weaknesses: Windows restrict global context sharing within a single layer; cross-window communication relies on window shifts in subsequent blocks
High implementation complexity compared to standard ViT
legacy: Popularized local window self-attention in Vision Transformers, enabling them to act as general-purpose backbones for standard downstream tasks.

title: A ConvNet for the 2020s
summary:
Problem Solved: The rapid rise of Vision Transformers led to the belief that standard CNNs had hit a performance wall and could not scale as effectively.
Significance: Extremely popular backbone in modern vision setups (like Stable Diffusion) due to its simplicity, speed, and robustness.
keyContributions: Modernized a standard ResNet with transformer design choices (7x7 depthwise convs, LayerNorm, GELU, patchify stems), proving that pure CNNs can achieve accuracy and scaling comparable to ViTs.
strengths: Maintains CNN speed, memory efficiency, and simple implementation while matching ViT accuracy
Doesn't require custom window attention kernels, facilitating deployment
weaknesses: Still bounded by local receptive fields (though larger 7x7) compared to global self-attention
Lacks the dynamic token-routing behavior of Transformers
legacy: Resurrected interest in pure convolutional backbones, showing that architectural training recipes and macro design choices are more critical than the attention vs. conv dichotomy itself.

title: MaxViT: Multi-Axis Vision Transformer
summary:
Problem Solved: Local window transformers (like Swin) lack true global receptive fields in a single block, while global transformers (like ViT) are too expensive.
Significance: State-of-the-art results on ImageNet classification and generative models, frequently used in competitive vision benchmark leaderboards.
keyContributions: Combined MBConv CNN blocks with block window attention (local context) and sparse grid attention (global context), achieving global self-attention in linear time.
strengths: Computes true global attention in linear time
Combines CNN inductive bias (MBConv) with Transformer flexibility
weaknesses: High parameter count and complex block structure
Slower training throughput compared to pure CNNs
legacy: Demonstrated that combining local convolutions with grid-based global attention creates highly efficient hybrid vision architectures.
